description: runtime-dominant host stream metric baseline
live runtime probe inside xctest still failed to create SLDisplayStream and did not improve the metric

raising the high-refresh raw skylight pending depth into a two-to-three frame range improved startup and drop pressure but still failed to recover progression enough to beat the best burst-window setup

lowering the hevc target quality to 0.34 did not beat the 192 mbps keep even though it nudged prores forward because the limiting hevc path stayed flat

raising the hevc idle replay ceiling from 240 hz to 300 hz reduced hevc progression and failed to beat the 192 mbps keep

a saturation-only latest-frame overflow for high-refresh hevc improved hevc latency a little but did not lift progression and regressed prores enough to lose to the pending-depth keep

copying replay HEVC sample buffers with fresh timing still reduced HEVC progression and left ProRes below the kept high-refresh stack

stop treating capture processing queue saturation as a decoder reset or restart trigger in Lumen ingress; this removed recovery entirely and caused severe backlog-driven latency and startup regressions
gate saturation-triggered resync on repeated packet stalls in Lumen ingress; this still let startup backlog run away and did not recover HEVC progression

handle startup queue saturation by requesting a fresh IDR without flushing ingress state or restarting the session; this improved over fully disabling resets but still left startup backlog and regression versus the best
lightly reset startup saturation backlog by flushing ingress and requesting a fresh startup IDR without a full decoder resync; this amplified startup drop churn and regressed far below the best

fast-forward startup ingress backlog toward the freshest queued recovery point while waiting for the first IDR packet; this removed drop churn and improved startup materially but still regressed the active best
combine startup backlog fast-forwarding with an immediate fresh IDR request when no queued recovery point survives; this substantially improved bring-up but still did not beat the active best
gate startup backlog fast-forwarding and fresh IDR requests behind observed startup queue pressure; this preserved the same bring-up pattern but still regressed the active best

clamp the shared encoded ingress to a single startup frame and restore steady-state capacity after the first accepted packet; this matched the best Lumen-side startup latest-wins result but still trailed the active best

clamp startup forwarding capacity in the bridge actor and restore steady-state capacity after the first encoded frame callback; this moved startup latest-wins earlier but restored too early to beat the active best
keep the bridge startup forwarding clamp active until the first startup key frame arrives; this held startup latest-wins too long and regressed HEVC with new drop churn
limit startup bridge forwarding clamp to high-refresh HEVC only and restore steady-state capacity after the first encoded frame; this still destabilized startup and regressed both codecs

teach the shared encoded ingress to keep only the freshest startup frame until a recovery point is queued and to end startup mode when that recovery point is consumed; this improved startup latency but reduced early progression below the active best
preserve a startup recovery point plus one following frame in the shared encoded ingress; this kept startup latency low but still did not recover enough early progression to beat the active best
widen the shared-ingress startup recovery continuity window to the recovery point plus two following frames; this improved early progression but still did not beat the active best
defer SkyLight synthetic idle replay until the first encoded output callback; this removed startup duplicate churn but reduced HEVC and ProRes progression below the active best

separate replay and recovery by forcing immediate keyframe requests to wait for fresh frames and never replay the last submitted frame through VT; this confirmed startup still lacks enough fresh early continuity to beat the active best

prioritize fresh frames over replay backlog by dropping replay admission whenever pending work exists; this build failed because the replay-origin metadata experiment had already been reverted from the baseline

split fresh, replay, and recovery flow by tagging frame origin, keeping recovery bound to the last fresh frame, and making replay low-priority admission; this proved replay still carries too much early continuity to deprioritize without hurting startup
move continuity replay after encode and mark replay frames in Lumen ingress; the first build crashed because the MDK origin-tagging stack was incomplete
continue the post-encode continuity replay experiment after restoring MDK origin tagging; the first retry still failed because bridge synthetic forwarding needed a default non-replay flag
move continuity replay after encode, tag replay frames through the bridge, and exempt replay-tagged duplicate payloads from recovery; this matched the active best but did not improve it

keep post-encode continuity replay but remove immediate encoder-side keyframe replay so recovery only latches the next fresh keyframe; this slightly reduced HEVC and ProRes progression below the active best

separate recovery replay from continuity replay by tagging fresh versus replay frames, pinning immediate keyframe recovery to the freshest real frame, and suppressing duplicate recovery replays for the same fresh frame

gate immediate recovery replay on fresh-frame age so very recent fresh frames absorb the pending keyframe request instead of triggering a synthetic replay; this reduced HEVC and ProRes progression below the active best

suppress redundant wait-for-IDR resets while an external keyframe request is already in flight so duplicate payload and saturation signals stop reflushing ingress state; this matched an older best but did not beat the current fresh-frame recovery replay keep

route replay-origin frames through an actor-backed latest-wins mailbox so only fresh frames consume synchronous pending admission while replay drains when capacity returns; this matched the active best but did not improve it

move continuity replay after encode on top of the fresh-frame recovery split and mark replay duplicates in ingress so only fresh output and immediate recovery stay on the VideoToolbox path; this improved structure clarity but scored below the active best
offload only synthetic timer replay after encode while keeping idle replay and fresh-frame recovery on the active-best VideoToolbox path; this matched the active best but did not improve it
use only completed fresh output as the recovery replay source instead of the last submitted fresh frame; this delayed recovery too much and collapsed early HEVC and ProRes progression

apply MaxFrameDelayCount=0 only during high-refresh HEVC startup and restore the steady-state delay budget after the first output callback; this still collapsed early HEVC and ProRes progression below the active best

apply a startup-only 360 Hz HEVC expected frame-rate and duration hint before the first successful output callback, then restore the steady-state 240 Hz hint after startup completes; this matched the older pacing-only HEVC gain but regressed versus the active fresh-frame recovery split

extend the startup-only 360 Hz HEVC expected frame-rate and duration hint across the first three successful output callbacks before restoring the steady-state 240 Hz hint; the longer startup pacing window reduced both HEVC and ProRes progression below the active best

skip steady-state HEVC data-rate limits for the first three successful startup outputs and restore them afterward so early output is not clamped by the long-run bit budget; this sharply reduced both HEVC and ProRes progression and closed the startup data-rate split axis

let immediate recovery replay force a keyframe without consuming the pending next-fresh keyframe request so the next real fresh submission also stays recovery-ready; this caused excessive keyframe pressure and collapsed both HEVC and ProRes startup progression

suppress synthetic recovery replay when a fresher real frame has already been submitted before the replay reaches the encode queue; even generation-accurate replay suppression collapsed startup continuity for both codecs

run immediate recovery replay synchronously on the encode queue instead of deferring it asynchronously so recovery work lands earlier; prioritizing replay timing this way still hurt startup continuity and both codec scores

allow only one idle or timer replay submission between successful output callbacks so continuity replay cannot flood the encoder path; continuity density turned out to be necessary and both codecs regressed sharply

add an actor-backed latest-fresh-frame mailbox at the source boundary so saturated fresh frames are retained and drained after pending capacity returns; preserving fresh frames this way still collapsed startup continuity and did not improve the active best

reduce HDR HEVC staging cost by letting the staged HDR HEVC path accept BGRA input while keeping Main10/HDR session properties; this caused private direct IOSurface capture warmup failure for both codecs and crashed the runtime probe

widen bridge forwarding capacity for >=120fps HDR paths by one slot and update bootstrap expectations; this compiled after bridging replay compatibility but regressed HEVC/ProRes progression and startup badly

reduce ProRes Proxy detached staging when direct submit is already valid and restore replay metadata contract for the local-package bridge; build succeeded but both HEVC and ProRes progression regressed versus the current best

restore replay metadata across the local MDK-to-Lumen package boundary so the local-package bridge builds against the fresh-frame recovery split; this re-enabled the build but measured far below the historical best

select a corrected local-package baseline by defaulting bridge-consumed MDK frames to non-replay against MDK best 6fe808a; this does not recover the historical 94.58 but restores a reproducible buildable baseline for continued autoresearch
let matched-format ProRes Proxy frames stay on the direct submission path on the corrected local baseline; this did not improve either codec and slightly reduced ProRes progression

avoid reapplying identical HDR image-buffer attachments on replayed frames by caching a per-buffer marker; this improved callback timings slightly but did not move fresh-frame progression or the combined score

deliver encoded frames directly from the VT output callback thread and leave the outputQueue for bookkeeping only; removing this callback-to-consumer hop did not improve fresh-frame progression on the corrected local baseline

release direct-path pending budget immediately after VT accepts an IOSurface-backed frame while leaving staged paths unchanged; this recovered ProRes startup/latency enough to raise the corrected local baseline
combine direct-path early pending release with direct VT callback delivery; once the callback hop was removed on top of the new baseline, both HEVC and ProRes startup regressed

restore replay provenance across the local MDK-to-Lumen boundary on top of the corrected direct-submit baseline; HEVC progression ticked up slightly but ProRes stayed flat and the combined score regressed

add one more high-refresh SkyLight callback-low-latency pending slot on top of the corrected direct-submit early-release baseline; this improved both HEVC and ProRes enough to raise the corrected best
add yet another high-refresh SkyLight callback-low-latency pending slot above 217; HEVC held while ProRes startup and latency improved further, raising the corrected best again

probe one more high-refresh callback-low-latency pending slot above experiment 218 by raising the callback budget to queueDepth+5/min7/max9; the added floor hurt startup/progression and fell back to experiment 217 levels

keep the experiment 218 startup floor of six callback frames but allow one extra high-refresh slot under pressure with queueDepth+5/min6/max9; this blew up HEVC startup and collapsed the combined score
keep experiment 218's wider callback budget for HEVC but relax ProRes back toward experiment 217; codec-specific pending budgets gave back too much ProRes and still failed to lift the combined score
keep experiment 218's cold-start callback budget at queueDepth one but expand more aggressively only after pressure appears; the pressure-only headroom merely fell back to experiment 217 levels
provide an explicit 240 Hz-style per-frame duration alongside the existing high-refresh HEVC session hints; VT still produced fewer useful outputs and regressed below the corrected best

move submitted-frame bookkeeping off the synchronous outputQueue round-trip so the encode hot path only enqueues the diagnostic counter update; this blew up HEVC startup and collapsed the combined score

revive the old high-refresh HDR HEVC staging-backlog cap by limiting only that codec/mode to four inflight staging slots while leaving ProRes wide open; this finally improved both HEVC and ProRes on the corrected baseline
raise the new high-refresh HDR HEVC staging-backlog cap from four slots to five; the extra slot gave back both HEVC and ProRes progress and proved four is the tighter sweet spot
pair the new four-slot HEVC staging cap with the older 217 callback pending budget; the staging cap and wider 218 callback budget turned out to be complementary, not redundant
tighten the new HEVC-only staging cap from four slots to three; the extra pressure shaved startup a bit but still gave back the combined score versus the four-slot keep

gating the actor-ordered drain on VT NumberOfPendingFrames lowered official HEVC callback latency to 41.876 ms but still produced 44 drop events, while local production source cadence fell further to about 32.61 fps, so polling intermediate pending state from the source side both reintroduces choke and still fails to satisfy the runtime probe stability contract

output-callback-driven drain credits preserved same-host HEVC source cadence at about 111.24 fps and cut local callback latency to about 21.16 ms, but mailbox replacements still surfaced as 82 queue-saturated drop events in the official probe, so intentional latest-wins coalescing must be separated from real overload failures before this structure can score

separating latest-wins coalescing from real dropped-frame failures eliminated the 425 stability penalty entirely, but the output-driven HEVC drain still landed at 49 frames with 390.162 ms startup and 42.847 ms average callback latency, so the remaining problem is startup/progression tuning inside the new credit model rather than event semantics

raising output-driven HEVC warmup credits from 2 to 3 cut HEVC startup to 335.808 ms but left HEVC stuck at 49 frames and collapsed ProRes Proxy progression to 7 frames, so extra warmup depth destabilizes the shared runtime instead of improving the new drain model

tightening the HEVC output-driven pending limit from 6 to 3 recovered HEVC to 50 frames without any stability penalty, but startup stayed at 356.676 ms and ProRes Proxy only reached 25 frames, so the new drain model still trails the 95.83 keep despite better backlog control

lowering the output-driven HEVC source pending limit from 3 to 2 improved startup to 311.729 ms and kept drop events at zero, but HEVC progression stayed capped at 50 frames and total score remained 95.42, so source-backlog tightening alone is no longer the limiting lever

classifying current best mailbox replacements as coalesced events did not preserve the 404 progression path; HEVC fell to 49 frames and ProRes Proxy to 23 frames, so the remaining bottleneck is stale work inside the decoupled drain model rather than drop-event accounting on the old path

replacing the ordered-drain backlog with a single latest queued frame did not improve the official score; HEVC stayed at 50 frames with 42.430 ms average callback latency, so stale queued frames inside the coordinator are not the dominant limiter in the output-driven model

granting an extra drain credit immediately after successful HEVC submit when VT pending<=1 slightly improved startup but raised HEVC callback latency to 62.835 ms and still left progression at 50 frames, so steady-state post-submit credit grants overdrive the pipeline without lifting the official score

limiting post-submit drain credits to bootstrap-only still poisoned the shared runtime: HEVC stayed at 50 frames but callback latency rose to 62.504 ms and ProRes Proxy fell to 25 frames with a 90.21 total score, so submit-return credit grants are the wrong control surface even when scoped to startup

actorizing the SkyLight source coordinator removed the serial delivery queue, but HEVC immediately picked up 6 drop events and collapsed to 83.42 despite the raw target-sized q2 source still measuring 115.40 fps locally, so source callback/replay actorization without a new ordering gate breaks the current keep path

splitting the SkyLight source bookkeeping queue from the processor submit queue removed callback-path coupling without destabilizing the runtime, but HEVC stayed pinned at 50 frames with 335.476 ms startup and 101.433 ms average callback latency, so queue conflation is not the dominant remaining limiter

moving 120 Hz ProRes Proxy overlay sessions from q1 to q3 on top of the kept raw SkyLight backend and wider pending slack cut drop pressure to seven events with zero dropped frames, raising ProRes to 82.51 and the official score to 95.62 while HEVC remained the limiting path at 51 frames

forcing 120 Hz HEVC overlay sessions onto q3 immediately broke the targeted bootstrap guardrails by changing the expected auto queue profile from q1 to q3 and raising the recommended forwarding frame capacity from 2 to 3, so explicit deeper HEVC source queueing is not compatible with the current official contract

adding one extra high-refresh HEVC raw SkyLight source slot improved ProRes stability as a side effect but left HEVC pinned at 51 frames and therefore could not lift the overall score beyond 95.62, so extra HEVC source slack alone is not the lever that breaks the current progression ceiling

dropping high-refresh HEVC MaxFrameDelayCount from 1 to 0 did not move the 51-frame HEVC ceiling and therefore could not lift the overall score beyond 95.62, so VT frame-delay buffering is not the missing lever on the current path

removing VideoToolbox data-rate limits from the high-refresh HEVC path left the 51-frame HEVC ceiling unchanged and only shifted ProRes stability, so HEVC rate smoothing is not the missing lever

MacDisplayKit: experiment: shared tile VT session with callback-gated tile source release; kept ~36ms callback latency but regressed to 96 complete groups with 13 HEVC drops

MacDisplayKit: experiment: stop raw warmup stream from first clean callback via async stop queue; regressed to 94 HEVC complete groups, 5 incomplete groups, 1 drop, and warmup callbacks returned to 2
MacDisplayKit: experiment: fast-exit first clean warmup callback before dirty-rect accounting; collapsed stability with 30 HEVC drops, 96 complete groups, and warmup callbacks increased to 3
MacDisplayKit: experiment: lower first-clean warmup queue depth to 1; reached 194 HEVC records and 97 complete groups with 0 incomplete groups but collapsed stability with 31 HEVC drops
MacDisplayKit: experiment: combine warmup queue depth 1 with post-clean callback suppression; reduced warmup callbacks to 1 but regressed to 95 complete groups with 25 HEVC drops
MacDisplayKit: experiment: combine per-lane tile serial queues with post-clean warmup suppression; queue wait fell near zero but HEVC regressed to 190 records, 94 complete groups, 2 drops
MacDisplayKit: experiment: sample VT NumberOfPendingFrames every 8 encoded submissions; hot-path VT pending polling regressed HEVC to 191 records, 94 complete groups, 3 incomplete groups, and 4 drops
MacDisplayKit: experiment: release HEVC tiled source frames after Metal staging; tied best with 192 HEVC records, 95 complete groups, 0 drops, but did not improve groups and ProRes fell to 133 frames

MacDisplayKit: experiment: combine shared HEVC tile VT session with post-clean warmup callback suppression; reduced warmup callbacks to 1 but regressed HEVC to 193 records, 96 complete groups, 1 incomplete group, and 1 drop

MacDisplayKit: experiment: raise shared HEVC tile VT session cadence hint from 240 to 360 on the clean warmup baseline; latency max stayed about 37 ms and HEVC regressed to 192 records, 96 complete groups, 5 drops

MacDisplayKit: experiment: force VideoToolbox low-latency rate control for shared HEVC tile session; official eval produced no parseable output after extended wait and was killed, so LLRC override is not a safe shared-session lever

MacDisplayKit: experiment: alternate shared HEVC tile submit order by source sequence; score tied only because of cap but HEVC regressed to 157 records, 78 complete groups, and 158 VT submissions despite lower avg callback latency, so ordered-submit balancing is not a safe shared-session lever

Lumen: experiment: measure encoded tile contract readiness in the runtime probe; HEVC showed 159 contract-ready tile updates and 0 invalid tile records, but strict groups stayed below target at 80 and the capped score only tied, so metadata is present but presentation contract remains unimplemented

Lumen: experiment: replay 2081-style tile phase presentation offline from a fixed trace buffer; HEVC stayed stable with 0 drops but produced only 8 two-frame updates and 158 three-frame updates, so the earlier two-frame recovery is not reproducible as a low-overhead post-capture contract
